sentences.text: All right.
There was something that I meant to mention way back when we were talking about my wonderful uncle and how much I relied on him for health care.
It was sort of taken for granted that he was always there.
And he was always going to sort of patch me up and look after me.
And I'm very aware that early in 1938, I had an appendicitis attack while we were still in Duren.
And it was a time when Gentile physicians really didn't treat Jewish patients anymore.
And it was pretty obvious to my uncle that I needed surgery for this emergency situation.
And I believe he-- he called in one of their favors that he did for one of his colleagues who was a surgeon.
I remember he had him called away from his hunting trip.
And when he appeared at my bedside to confirm that we needed to do surgery immediately, he was still in his hunting gear.
And I, for many, many years after the-- in my memory, I could still sort of somewhat visualize and smell the woods where-- because he-- he was fresh out of-- out of the woods.
And-- but he did, he did perform the surgery.
And I'm grateful for that.
Because one of my very dear friends who also left Duren was caught in the same situation.
She was on a children's transport.
And they were stopping over in Switzerland.
And she had an emergency situation appendicitis.
And there was no medical facility on the spot.
And she died.
So that-- it sort of takes on a very important moment in my life, how things can go well and how things can go wrong in many, many ways when you're uprooted and sort of put out into the world.
At any rate, to come back to the farm issue, we were always aware that we needed to be careful-- keep out of sight, not-- not be identified, and that sort of thing.
And one morning, early in the morning, I came in from my pasture.
And all the women were standing in the kitchen.
And they were very-- seemed very nervous.
My mother looked particularly pale.
And as I came into the room, I realized there were two German soldiers standing in the kitchen, questioning them and not getting very far because they were speaking German.
And finally, in, exasperation, somebody said in French, anybody understand me, or whatever?
And my little farmer's friend, farmer's daughter, my little friend, spoke up and pointed to me and said, she speaks German, at which point I think everybody turned very pale.
And the soldier said, is that true?
Do you speak German?
And of course, I had no choice but to admit that I did.
And the project for the day and the problem for the day was that they were looking for carrier pigeons which had gotten lost.
They assumed they were in some farmer's loft, mixed in with other pigeons.
And it was their job to find these pigeons.

And after looking through the loft at our farmer's farm, they said, well, they were going to take me along and have me do the interpreting for the day or however long it was going to take to find the pigeons, at which point my mother's knees buckled.
And off I went at the age of 12 with-- not quite 12, but almost 12-- with these two German soldiers on a motorbike, a motorcycle with a sidecar.
And we went looking for pigeons.
And of course, my mother was absolutely convinced that I was not going to come back.
And I believe the farmer's wife also felt that this was not a very good situation.
At any rate, with the soldiers who were quite civil-- and I had made up my mind as I got into this very tight spot-- that they were young soldiers.
And I was going to talk to them the way I would have talked to my cousin and hang loose, limit my German to the necessary things, and do most of it in French, fluent French.
As we went around the countryside, of course, the first thing they asked is how come I spoke German out of all these people there?
And I said that I-- I had a German grandmother who used to speak to me in German.
And that is why I knew the language.
And so we went from farm to farm all day long and questioned about the pigeons, searched the loft.

And of course, now as an adult, as I look back, I'm absolutely certain that had there been carrier pigeons, the French farmers would have hid them anyway and not had them in their loft available for Germans to pick up.
But it-- it seemed like a fairly serious task.
And we were gone all day.
And it was dark when they were finally decided to end the day.

They bought me a very long French bread as a reward for my efforts and brought-- brought me back to the farm, and where everybody stood in disbelief, my mother, of course, her face tear-stained and-- and super-apprehensive, and my father as well.
But there I was, shipshape and back.
And although it was a very frightening experience, it came to a, and OK ending.
And then shortly thereafter, the word went forth that anybody was not a French citizen had to return to their place of origin.
And these were signs on trees and poles or whatever there might have been.
There was no escaping this edict.

And my father again pondered and pondered and decided there was no way that he was going to go back anywhere-- not Brussels or anywhere else-- and decided again if there was any way-- of course, France had capitulated at that point.
It is now the end of July, 1940.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father still felt, even though probably the relatives would have been gone from Paris, that he was going to try for Paris once more and to pick up some sort of a connection.
And so first, he went out and we sampled bicycles for two for my father and my mother.
Because my mother couldn't ride a bicycle.
But then it was really so difficult and my brother couldn't master a bicycle.
So we aborted that scheme.
And it was a short train ride from Boulogne-sur-Mer to Lille, which was the nearest big city.
And with trepidations, we boarded the train.
And we went to Lille.
In Lille, again, it was not a quiet town.
We-- we stayed in a chintzy hotel.
And during the night, there was a gunshot through the window-- not directed at us, I'm sure, but just some scuffle that was going on in town.
And then after a day in Lille, my father said he was going somewhere to meet someone.
And he would be back.
And my mother and we children waited until my mother could stand it no longer and went to the meeting place where she was absolutely certain my father had been dealt with in an unkind of way.
And we went to this old, old storage area near the harbor, which was dark and smelly and horrible.
And there was my father.
And his contact had just left.
And the transaction was that my father paid him probably out of some of the jewelry that he had tucked away and this man drove an ambulance from Lille to Paris, which was a fairly good distance.
And for a price, he would take us under the cover, a compartment that was closed over, he would take us in this compartment in ambulance and deposit us in Paris.
And so we did, again, with a little bit of nourishment-- not much, because you couldn't get any.
Everything was rationed.
We set out on this journey.
And indeed, with some uncomfortable interruptions, he brought us to Paris.
Once we were in Paris, my father found out there was no one there that he, that he knew of the family.
And so we stopped somewheres in an outdoor cafe, which looked a little tempting for a cup of coffee or so.
Coffee wasn't what coffee is now.
And while we were sitting there, at adjoining table, there were two women, obviously refugees, speaking with a heavy German accent, a Viennese accent.
And at some point, my father brought up the courage to address them.
And the one woman said, yes, we-- my husband is very active in helping refugees.
And he'll be, he'll be here in about five minutes.
And then he will assist you.
And this was our next guardian angel.
Because he and his wife were Romanian Jewish refugees.
They had no children.
And he made it his mission to get us on the road to our destination.
Because he kept saying to my father, you have children.
You have to make it.
They have to live.
We're-- we're old.
We have no family.
It's unimportant.
And so he gave my father money for whatever he needed.
Their name was Spitter, Leon Spitter.
And I think the wife was Henrietta Spitter.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he went with my father as soon as the American consulate reopened to telegraph my family here in United States in New York, tell them that we were alive and that we were in dire need of money, as well as to again start working on the visa.
Because all of our papers, when Antwerp and the lowlands were-- were bombed, the American consulate not only was bombed, but everything was lost.
So there was no trace of our visa or our tickets in order to sail.
There was no one to say, yes, indeed, we were headed in that direction.
We had to start really from square one, so to speak, at, a at the very beginning.
In the meantime, my good deed-- I had two deeds to do.
One of them was to look after Mrs.
Spitter, who was diabetic and couldn't resist French pastry and used to go out.
And I had to sit with her.
And she would invariably have a diabetic coma or something.
She would fall off the chair.
And it was my job to see to it that she got home.
We lived in a boarding house.
We had a furnished room.
The bath was in the hallway-- very French, very old, very dirty.
[LAUGHS] And-- but there was a refugee physician and his wife from Vienna who lived there as well.
And he sort of shepherded over people who needed medical attention.
And the other full-time job that I had was to queue up as a bedraggled, little French girl, which I was because I had no clothing to speak of, and queue up all day long, from one queue to the next.
I started early in the morning when the stores opened at 7:00 and queued up for bread, queued up for milk, queued up for sometimes even an egg or two.
And that was, it was a full-time occupation.
Because that was the only way you could really get food in-- in occupied Paris at the time.
There was no glamor.
The Louver was sealed.
The Eiffel Tower was closed.
All places of-- of culture were closed.
And it was this city under siege, absolutely.
And so there we were from the beginning of August.
And finally, finally got our papers, our exit visas and-- and our passage for America early in December, December 6, if I'm correct, 1940.
And of course, the only way that we could get to our harbor at the time-- it had to be a neutral harbor.
It had to be a neutral ship that could sail across the Atlantic Ocean.
And so the only way to go was to take a train to the border of France to Spain and then traverse Spain all the way across and then into Portugal and go to Portugal and get a ship out of Lisbon.
When we finally left Paris, there was a really emotional farewell to all our friends, and especially to this Leon Spitter who had helped us so very much.
And at the border from France to Spain, the German-- I guess at that point, they were SS really, not SR anymore-- did a body search.
My mother and I were to put into one room and had a-- had to strip and had a very thorough body search for not smuggling anything out of the country.
And at the end of it, I was still wearing some rubber boots that I was wearing because the weather was foul.
And the SS man who was standing there with a whip, actually, confronted me across the room about I hadn't taken off my boots.
What was I hiding in them?
And in a fit of fury, I kicked off the boot which was fairly loose on my foot.
And it went flying across the room and just nearly hit him in the face.
My mother thought, oh, god.
This is it.
This is the end.
They're not going to let us go.
And it took him a moment to get his composure.
And then he smiled in a very sardonic kind of way.
And he said, OK.
Doesn't make any difference anyway.
He said, they're going to send you back.
You'll be back here.
You're not going to get out.
But we-- we-- the paper said OK.
And so we got onto the-- the train through Spain, which was an old, rickety-rackety train with very sparse provisions, because we were going to be on the train a day and night and a day.
And it was raining.
And we sat with the-- holding umbrellas, because the rain was coming through the roof of-- of the train.
I mean, there was no relief from the weather.
It's cold, damp.
There were others.
I believe there were 10.
And we, you know, traveled together, so to speak.
And we got to the Portuguese border.
And seven of us were allowed to go into Portugal without papers.
The rest were really sent back.
And I remember tears and lamentations and so forth that the others could not enter Portugal as well.
And we entered Portugal and traveled to Lisbon.
And once again, in Lisbon, made contact with a very good friend of this Leon Spitter, who put us in touch with the proper organizations and was helpful to my father in many ways.
We lived in a, again, a rented, dirty room.
And daily, my father went out to work on the legal part of things.
And together with other refugees that had been in Lisbon already waiting and not getting very far, they pooled the money.
My uncle sent more money from New York.
And for a sizable amount, I'm sure, convinced a, a captain, a ship's captain, would he take us to New York?
This was very interesting, because this captain had never sailed across the Atlantic.
He had a very small boat, which in Lisbon was called a banana boat.
And all it had ever done was gone from Lisbon to the Azores to pick up bananas and bring them back to Lisbon.
He had never gone across the ocean.
But as they say, money talks.
They convinced him.
And we sailed out of Lisbon December 26 under full lights, because it was a neutral ship.
It almost looked like a carnival, because there were lights all over the place.
The year was December 1940.
And we set sail.
Very early on, because there were mines in the ocean, we never got out of our life jackets.
And because I was so terribly seasick, I never made it from the deck to anywhere else-- to the cabin or whatever place.
I just sat up there.
And they fed me oranges.
And then there was an enormous winter storm on the Atlantic.
Our engines cut out and wouldn't work anymore.
And we were just bobbing along and bobbing along until the ship almost drifted as far south as northern Florida before the engines could be fixed.
And then we steamed up the coast to New York.

In the meantime, of course, while we were out in the ocean bobbing around, there were, almost every other night, some sort of a U-boat, U-boat would come out of the black Atlantic while I was on the deck there.

And to this day, I've never really gotten over this horrible sensation of being on a boat in the middle of the water and bloop, bloop, bloop, these-- these monstrous things would somehow come up from the depth of-- of the ocean.
And they would board us.
And they would examine papers.
And they would look for contraband.
And everybody was questioned.
And then they would go away.
And it was alternated between the German U-boats and the British U-boats.
The-- the-- the-- the German U-boats, they-- whoever boarded, they were very brisk and demanded to speak to people.
And my father tried to blend into the background.
I'm sure other people did as well.
I was told to seal my lips.
And [LAUGHS] I wonder why.
And the-- the-- the British U-boats were a little less threatening.
But nevertheless, we never knew until they were actually up and alongside of our boat who was boarding us and how the winds of war were blowing.
So it was a-- a very harrowing experience crossing the Atlantic.
And I have, to this day, not been able really to ever think in terms of crossing the Atlantic on a ship again.
And I-- I guess that's one of the things that has remained with me that-- that awful sensation and fear.
And there we were in New York and found ourselves in the bosom of my mother's family.
We landed January 8, 1941, on my brother's birthday, which was another momentous occasion.
And my mother's brother was at the pier to meet us.
And we were to split up.
I was to live with an uncle who had two daughters, one older and one younger than myself by a year and a half.
And my brother was going with a different uncle who had a young son.
And it would be compatible for the two boys to sleep together and share a room.
And my parents were going to take a furnished room.
And they would have to immediately go to work, my mother doing housework, my father sweeping floors at Klein's department store.
And I remember rather vividly my uncle heading home with me to-- to-- he lived out on the island in a small town.
And when we came out of the subway at the Flushing station, there was a huge fruit and vegetable stand with great grapefruits and oranges and wonderful fruit.

And I stood in front of it in-- in my weird clothing that said, you know, I'm from Europe, I'm from somewhere far away, and just wept and wept and cried and sobbed at the sight of all this wonderful food.
And my uncle was so embarrassed.
He walked a block away.
He didn't want to be associated with me or-- or even acknowledge that this little urchin who was weeping over fresh fruit, in any shape, form, or fashion, belonged to him.
Because it was an embarrassment.
And that was my introduction to the United States.
I could not speak one word of English.
I didn't even know yes and no.
And once again, had to start way, way at the bottom, at the beginning, for another language, my fourth language at this point in my 12 and a half years.
And it was difficult coming to this-- this town which was called Whitestone, which was beyond Flushing, New York, because it was a-- a small Yankee town.
I don't believe they had any notion whatsoever about the war in Europe.
They complained a lot about no coffee and silk stockings, which was rather unbelievable.
I couldn't imagine how anybody could get so excited about no coffee and no silk stockings.
But in general, I don't believe there were any Jewish families.
They had no knowledge of refugees or what refugees had gone through.
In fact, I think, since the United States was not in the war, they felt this-- this was something far away.
And they really did not know a great deal about it.
So I was a fish out of water.
There was no one I could talk to.
There was nobody who was understanding or sympathetic.
I mean, here I was in a very strange situation, more than happy to be alive but thoroughly confused.

And the next six weeks, I believe I cried every night, cried myself to sleep before I felt I had reached a point where I belonged and gradually eased myself into the American scene with the effort to blend or assimilate as quickly as possible.
No one knew there was German in my background.
To everyone, I passed as French.
I spoke French in school I did not want anybody to know.
I remember a teacher actually confronting me and saying, make up your mind.
Are you Jewish?
Or are you French?
We'll stop here, change tape.
Thank you.
